1:
Superscription: 'A Song for the Dedication of the Temple' (chanukkat ha-bayit) — chanukkah means dedication/consecration, making this the proto-text of the Hanukkah festival (1 Macc Psalms 4:59; 2 Macc 1:9 links the rededication of the Maccabean temple to the original dedication). The individual lament-to-praise structure made the psalm suitable for the dedication context: personal rescue parallels the sanctuary's restoration after defilement. 'Of David' in the superscription is unusual given David did not build the temple — it may indicate Davidic authorship repurposed for a later dedication event.

'You have lifted me up' (dillitani) — dalah (to draw up, as from a well) — the rescue metaphor is hydraulic: being pulled up out of the pit (the underworld, Sheol). The temple dedication context invites reading this as both individual recovery from illness and corporate recovery from national crisis.
2: 'I cried to you for help and you healed me' — the explicit healing language (rapha) suggests the psalm's original context was recovery from serious illness. In the ANE, illness and divine punishment were closely linked: disease was the deity's arrow (cf. Psalms 38:2; Job 6:4; Baal's seven arrows of disease). The sick person had typically been 'abandoned' by their protective deity, and recovery was restoration of the divine-human relationship. Healing therefore required both medical treatment and religious ritual (offering, lament psalm).
3: 'You brought my soul up from the realm of the dead; you restored my life from among those who descend to the pit' — Sheol and bor (pit) are the two standard Hebrew terms for the underworld. ANE underworld theology (Mesopotamian Kur/Aralu, Canaanite Mot's realm) depicted it as a place of dust, darkness, and the weak shadows of the dead. The psalmist experienced illness as descent into Sheol's territory; recovery is literal resurrection from that borderland. This makes the psalm structurally a 'resurrection narrative' — the individual death-and-return experience that later provides typological language for Christ's resurrection.
4: 'Sing praise to the LORD, you his faithful ones (chasidim)' — the chasidim (plural of chasid) are the covenant-loyal community, those who practice chesed (covenant faithfulness). The term appears frequently in the Psalter as a self-designation of the devout community (Psalms 4:3; Psalms 12:1; Psalms 16:10; Psalms 31:23; Psalms 37:28). The invitation to communal praise follows the ANE pattern of the recovered worshiper holding a thanksgiving feast and inviting the community to participate — the healed person's story becomes communal praise.
5: 'His anger is for a moment; his favor is for a lifetime. Weeping may tarry for the night, but joy comes in the morning' — the contrast of divine wrath (brief) and divine favor (a lifetime) is the psalm's theological thesis. ANE lament traditions often focused on the prolonged anger of the gods and the uncertainty of restoration; Ps 30 insists on YHWH's anger as limited and his chayim (life/favor) as permanent. The night/morning contrast was liturgically significant: the temple may have hosted vigils where lament was offered at night and praise in the morning (Psalms 5:3; Psalms 59:16).
6: 'In my prosperity I said, I shall never be moved' — the retrospective confession of the prosperity illusion. ANE wisdom texts (Proverbs, Egyptian Instruction texts) warn against the arrogance of success: Ptahhotep's Maxims, the Instruction of Amenemope, and Israelite wisdom all diagnose prosperity-pride as the root of disaster. The psalmist's 'I shall not be moved' (bal-emot) echoes the language of the just person in Psalms 1:3 and Psalms 15:5 — but here it is the arrogance of self-sufficiency rather than the confidence of trust.
7: 'By your favor, O LORD, you made my mountain stand strong; you hid your face and I was terrified' — YHWH's 'face-hiding' (hisatarta panecha) is one of the most significant theological concepts of the Psalter. In ANE patron-deity relationships, the god's 'face' (presence) was the source of all blessing; the hidden face meant withdrawal of protection, leaving the person exposed to disaster. The mountain imagery (har) may allude to the temple mount as YHWH's dwelling, or to the individual's secure situation. Strength comes from divine favor, not personal achievement.
8: 'To you, O LORD, I called; to the LORD I pleaded for mercy' — the account of the prayer offered during the crisis. The lament psalm was the standard liturgical form for such prayer: specific ritual texts were used at the sanctuary when approaching YHWH in distress. The psalmist's prayer in vv9-10 is a negotiation argument — appealing to YHWH's interests in maintaining the psalmist's life.
9: 'What profit is there in my death, in my going down to the pit? Will the dust praise you? Will it declare your faithfulness?' — the argument from divine self-interest: YHWH benefits from keeping the psalmist alive because the dead cannot praise him. This remarkable pragmatic argument assumes the Psalter's anthropology that the dead in Sheol do not have full relationship with YHWH (Psalms 6:5; Psalms 88:10-12; Psalms 115:17). The argument is later answered in Isaiah 26:19 (resurrection) and definitively in the NT resurrection.
10: 'Hear, O LORD, and be merciful to me; O LORD, be my helper' — the three divine names/titles in this verse create an intensified plea: YHWH (covenant name) + chonneni (show grace, appeal to his nature) + YHWH (again) + ezer (helper, the Eden-word: Genesis 2:18, 'ezer suitable for him'). The threefold address is a liturgical intensification pattern common in lament psalms.
11: 'You have turned my mourning into dancing; you have removed my sackcloth and clothed me with joy' — saq (sackcloth, goat-hair cloth) was worn as a physical enactment of mourning and petition — the rough texture signaled distress to both humans and the deity. Removing sackcloth was the physical cessation of mourning. Dancing (machol) was a liturgical practice at the sanctuary (Psalms 149:3; Psalms 150:4; Jeremiah 31:13). The clothing imagery (removing sackcloth, being clothed with joy) is a vestment metaphor: divine rescue changes the worshiper's liturgical garment.
12: 'That my glory may sing your praise and not be silent. O LORD my God, I will give thanks to you forever' — kavodi (my glory) — possibly the liver/soul as seat of honor, or the psalmist's reputation in the community. The restored person's public praise is the completion of the rescue: honor is restored through public thanksgiving. The vow 'I will give thanks forever' concludes what likely was a thanksgiving offering ceremony at the temple, where the recovered person brought a todah (thanksgiving) sacrifice and recited the psalm publicly (Psalms 22:25; Psalms 116:14).
